symbols: flaming sword — the divine weapon of heavenly justice against evil
shield with cross — the protection of God and the victory of Christ's cross over darkness
golden armor — the full armor of God, divine authority for the angelic warrior
fiery abyss below — hell, the destination of the defeated dragon and rebellious angels
wings spread wide — the celestial power and authority of the archangel
description: A tall arched composition showing a winged warrior angel descending from above. The figure wears golden-bronze armor with a flowing red cloak, wielding a sword in one hand and a shield in the other. Large white-gray wings spread behind. Below, a dark fiery landscape suggests hell or an abyss with reddish-orange flames. The angel's expression is resolute and commanding. The upper portion shows clouds and a pink-red sky. The entire composition conveys dynamic downward movement.
theological_description: The Archangel Michael is heaven's supreme warrior, whose name means 'Who is like God?' — a rhetorical challenge to Satan's pride. Revelation describes the cosmic battle: 'Michael and his angels fought against the dragon, and the dragon and his angels fought back. But he was not strong enough, and they lost their place in heaven' (Revelation 12:7-8). The flaming sword and shield embody the 'full armor of God' that Paul describes (Ephesians 6:13-17) — heavenly realities of which the apostle's teaching is an earthly application. Jude records that 'even the archangel Michael, when he was disputing with the devil about the body of Moses, did not himself dare to condemn him, but said, "The Lord rebuke you!"' (Jude 1:9) — his authority comes not from himself but from God. Daniel names Michael as 'the great prince who protects your people' (Daniel 12:1), the guardian of Israel in cosmic conflict.

scripture_refs.reason: Michael and his angels fighting the dragon — the cosmic battle depicted
Michael the great prince who protects God's people
The full armor of God — realized in Michael's sword, shield, and armor
Michael disputing with the devil — his authority derived from God, not from himself
